titleStmt.title: Letter from William Upton Tyrrell to Elizabeth Tyrrell 16, June, 1917
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: William Upton Tyrrell

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: William Upton Tyrrell expresses gratitude for a parcel containing cake and lemonade.

msIdentifier.repository: OPW-Maynooth University Archive & Research Centre at Castletown

correspAction.placeName: Ballindoolin House, Ballindoolin, Edenderry, Kings Co., Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
16 June 1917. My dearest Mamy A thousand thanks for the lovely parcel
which came yesterday. Really you are more
than good. You have no idea how I love
them and everything is so delicious. That
cake is lovely and comes, in, I think better
condition that the fruit cake. The last two
parcels were particularly acceptable as up here
it is not easy to get extra stuff. Those sweets two are lovely and the
lemonade A1 in this hot weather. It is
impossible to find a cool place anywhere. You speak of cold in your letters.
I wish we had some of it. In that Irish Life you sent I see an
account of the death of Col. T. V. P. Mc Common
and they have put in a photo of W.G.C.
McCammond of the 3rd and called it
T.V P Mc Cammon. I wonder what does
he think of it. I am very glad to see that Bobby
is sticking up for his own as
described in his P S to his letter He
is developing the offensive spirit. There is not much news as you
can imagine. We are in camp well
behind and I expect will be going
right back. Will write soon again but not
tomorrow as there is nothing to
write about. With very best love from
Your most affec Son
Willie
